paragraphs.text: As an avid consumer of several entertainment media online (and in digital format), an important item concerning the concept of Digital Fairness is the preservation of all the media material and works we have purchased in several digital stores belonging to diverse (but related) industries, such as media/entertainment, music, publishing and videogames. We need legislative tools and certifications to make sure that our purchases are preserved for the future, even after the original publisher could (legitimately) cease any form of support. We need a standard and certified baseline for the definition a knowledge transfer at the ceasing time of a medium, regardless if it is a videogame, a digital book, a film, a tv series, etc. The original publisher won't have any obligations on performing a knowledge transfer by themselves, but to comply to a standardised process. The EU could introduce the definition of certification for software standards which could enable and facilitate the end of life management for said products and digital content. And publishers could certify their compliance to said standards, in a way that the product ownership could be safely kept at the consumer who legitimately purchased said digital content. This legislative tool would not have principles, definitions and specifications different than other tools that the European Union has successfully implemented in the Member Countries, always concerning the online safety of their Citizens, such as for instance the Global Data Protection Regulation, which gave all the EU Citizens the right to safely claim ownership of the data they can share to any company online, and which is currently in place in all the IT systems in the Member Countries.
